headline: Audit: Isolated Incidences of PERS spiking
content:
Pension inflation by PERS participants appears isolated and to have had a negligible effect on the fiscal health of Oregon's retirement system, according to an audit released today by Secretary of State Kate Brown.
http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3760505

The audit reviewed compensation data for all 2010-2013 PERS retirees (except Money Match recipients, whose pensions cannot be inflated), and compared compensation growth rates against the actuarially-assumed rate of 3.75% per year. While half of 2010 Public Employees Retirement System retirees had compensation growth that exceeded that rate, the number dropped to about one-fourth for 2013 retirees. In addition, compensation growth during the three-year period used to calculate pensions dropped from 4 percent for 2010 retirees to 1.6 percent for 2013 retirees.

Auditors also reviewed a sample of records with compensation increases and found that the majority were explainable by promotions, emergency overtime or other causes attributable to the normal course of work. Of the few remaining instances of above-average compensation growth rates, auditors did not determine whether any involved retirees may have intentionally contrived to obtain larger pensions.

“PERS costs have been a key issue for the state, local governments, and school districts in the wake of the Great Recession,” Secretary Brown said. “As the economy continues to recover, Oregon needs to ensure that PERS costs remain manageable and that state and local workers are fairly compensated for the valuable work they do.”

Pension inflation, sometimes called “pension spiking,” occurs when an employer increases an employee's final pension payout by increasing the amount of salary used to calculate his or her pension. Often, these increases in compensation are legitimate and commonplace. If numerous employees inflate their pensions, however, they can drive up the compensation costs and negatively affect the fiscal health of an organization or even an entire pension system.

Some employers are taking active steps to reduce the possibility of pension inflation within their organizations by limiting vacation accruals, reducing cost of living increases or using automated overtime distribution systems.

While public employers ultimately have responsibility for ensuring that pension inflation does not negatively impact their finances, the audit recommends that PERS consider helping employers by periodically analyzing employer compensation data for signs of pension inflation and communicating with employers if patterns appear.

The PERS audit is available on the Secretary of State website: http://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/default.aspx

headline: Independent Party Achieves Major Party Status
content:
Secretary of State Kate Brown today announced that the Independent Party of Oregon has achieved major party status as of Feb. 2, 2015. Major party status is subject to a statutorily required re-verification on the 274th day before the next Primary Election.

Oregon law requires that a major political party have membership totalling at least 5 percent of the number of registered voters in the most recent General Election. Based on the official results of the 2014 General Election, a major political party must have at least 108,739 members.

On Feb. 2, the Independent Party of Oregon had 108,742 members. On that date, a party official inquired about the process for the Secretary of State to officially determine that the party had met the major party threshold.

Accordingly, as of Feb. 2, 2015, the Independent Party of Oregon is a major political party in Oregon, subject to the re-verification of that status as a major political party on Aug. 17, 2015.

You can register to vote through the Secretary of State Elections Division: https://secure.sos.state.or.us/orestar/vr/register.do?lang=eng&source=SOS

content:
Secretary of State Kate Brown today released a report detailing the first year of work by the Oregon Office of Small Business Assistance.

The Office of Small Business Assistance worked on 140 cases for small, for-profit businesses with 100 or fewer employees in 2014, its first year in existence. When Secretary Brown proposed legislation to create the Office of Small Business in 2013, she said that a key to success would be its independence from any regulatory agency. headline: Oregon Constitution on Display
content:
Secretary of State Kate Brown is launching a fund-raising campaign to restore and regularly display Oregon's original 158-year-old Constitution. The fund-raising goal of $60,000 is to pay for necessary repairs and to purchase a secure, climate-controlled public display case.

To kick off the drive, the  Constitution will be on display in the Capitol Rotunda to commemorate the anniversary of statehood on Friday, Feb. 13 from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. and on Saturday, Feb. 14 from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m.

The document was written during a convention held in Salem in August and September of 1857. The Constitution was submitted to the Oregon Territory's voters on Nov. 9, 1857. The Territory's electorate, consisting of white men over the age of 21, voted in favor of the constitution 7,195 to 3,195.

Submitted to the U.S. Congress, it struggled to gain enough votes for passage but finally prevailed in early 1859. Oregon's Constitution was signed by President Buchanan on Feb. 14, 1859, making Oregon the 33rd state in the Union.

Only one other copy of the document exists, and it's part of the National Archives holdings in Washington, D.C. The Oregon Historical Society has the engrossed or draft version of the Constitution from which the adopted Constitution was written. Because of the age and fragile condition of the adopted Constitution, it's only displayed for limited amounts of time.

In its earlier days, the Constitution wasn't handled very carefully. In 1880, Secretary of State R.P. Earhart found it rolled up in the back of a safe in his office. Today, the binding is breaking. It's dirty (it survived the Capitol Building fire in 1935), and several torn pages need to be repaired.

Once the restoration is complete, the State Archives plans to display the Constitution more frequently, which will require a display case with glass that will filter out harmful light. It must also be secure and maintain constant temperature and humidity.

Get more information on the history of Oregon's Constitution.
http://sos.oregon.gov/archives/exhibits/constitution/Pages/default.aspx

View high-resolution images of the original document: https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/state-constitution.aspx
Make a donation to the Constitution Fund: Oregon State Archives, 800 Summer St. N.E., Salem, OR 97310.

headline: The 2015-2016 Oregon Blue Book Is Online
content:
The online version of the 2015-2016 Oregon Blue Book is now available: https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/default.aspx

The online Blue Book includes more than 2,000 pages of photos, histories, trivia, quizzes and other information about Oregon government, history and culture.

This edition of the Blue Book features a cover from Lithia Park in Jackson County, which is one of the 20 counties that have never appeared on the cover.

A feature of the new Blue Book is a series of essays written by Oregon schoolchildren. https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/explore/exhibits/essays-2015/introduction.aspx
The students were asked to describe why they like living in Oregon and their favorite places to visit. Including the essays will give Oregonians 100 years from now great insight into the hearts and minds of this generation. If the essays are any indication, Oregon's future is in good hands.

We also asked all of the state agencies to look at their entries and rewrite them with a focus on their mission and core business functions. The Oregon economy and government finance sections were also rewritten to use plain language to explain complicated subjects. New graphics are included. Also updated: The timeline of Oregon's history, to reflect some important dates that were previously not included such as the adoption of Oregon's public accommodation law in 1953 and the Oregon Equality Act in 2007.

The color insert in this year's printed Blue Book explores Oregon's early rural schools. The feature, which also is viewable online, uses photos, artwork and memorabilia to highlight the colorful history of rural education in Oregon before World War II.
https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/explore/exhibits/education-home.aspx

headline: Schoolchildren asked to help restore Oregon Constitution
content:
The State Archives invites Oregon’s schoolchildren to be part of history by helping to restore and regularly display Oregon’s Constitution.

The original state Constitution from 1857 is weathered and its binding is in need of repair. Because of security and preservation concerns, the Constitution is kept locked away from public view except on rare occasions.

Oregon’s current generation of schoolchildren have a chance to change that.

By participating in the Oregon Constitution Challenge, kids can help restore aOregon Constitutionnd regularly display Oregon’s original state Constitution by funding a professional restoration of the historic document and a secure, climate-controlled display case for regular public viewing at the State Archives in Salem.

The Oregon Constitution Challenge
Approximately $60,000 is needed to restore and regularly display the Oregon Constitution. Each school that raises a total of $250 or more by May 31 toward that goal will have its school name engraved on a plaque displayed with the original Constitution, so that every future generation to view the Constitution will appreciate the contribution.

The Oregon Constitution Challenge is open to all public, private and charter schools throughout Oregon. There are more than 600,000 schoolchildren in Oregon. If each student contributes just 10 cents each, then Oregon’s schoolchildren can put this project on a path to success.

In the early 1980s, Oregon’s schoolchildren helped raise money to regild the Oregon Pioneer on top of the Capitol building in Salem. That effort helped engage a generation of Oregon schoolchildren in protecting and preserving an enduring symbol of our state and its democracy.

Following on that effort, the State Archives hopes to engage another generation of Oregon schoolchildren in protecting and preserving our state Constitution. The Constitution is both an important symbol of our democracy and the founding legal document upon which Oregon was built. It deserves to be restored and regularly displayed, and Oregon’s schoolchildren are invited to help in that effort.

Oregon’s Constitution was drafted in 1857 and has had been through a lot in its lifespan so far.  In 1880, for example, Oregon’s Secretary of State found it rolled up in the back of a safe, and later the Constitution survived the fire that burned down Oregon’s earlier Capitol building in 1935.

Civics education
 During the fundraising drive, Oregon schoolchildren and their teachers are invited to consider the following civics education questions about Oregon’s Constitution and their state government.  In addition, every student submitting a written response to these questions will have their answers permanently added to the holdings at the Oregon State Archives.
1. How many branches of state government were established by the Oregon Constitution?  What are they, and what do they do?
2. How many statewide elected officers were established by the original Oregon Constitution?  Why do you think the framers of the Constitution established these different statewide offices?
3. The Oregon Constitution establishes a state Senate with how many members, and a state House of Representatives with how many members? How does the size of the Oregon Legislature established by the Oregon Constitution compare to other states and to the U.S. Congress? How do you think the size of the Oregon Legislature affects how the members of the Legislature work together?
4. Where did the Constitution set the location of the state capital? Why did the framers in 1857 locate the capital where they did?
5. Does the Oregon Constitution have its own Bill of Rights? How is it similar, and how is it different, than the Bill of Rights in the U.S. Constitution?
6. The Oregon Constitution was written in 1857, just before the start of the Civil War. How was the issue of slavery and African-American migration to Oregon addressed by the framers of Oregon’s Constitution? What progress ha
content2:
s Oregon made on the issue of civil rights over the more than 150 years since the Constitution was adopted?
7. What is the initiative and referendum system in Oregon? When was it added to the Oregon Constitution, and why?

Learn more about Oregon’s Constitution on the State Archives website: http://sos.oregon.gov/archives/exhibits/constitution/Pages/default.aspx

headline: Gov. Brown Appoints Jeanne Atkins as Oregon Secretary of State
content:
Gov. Kate Brown announced today that she has appointed Jeanne Atkins as Oregon Secretary of State. Atkins will be sworn in at 1 p.m. Wednesday, March 11, in the Governor’s Ceremonial Office at the Capitol.

“Jeanne Atkins will serve Oregon with integrity, transparency and good sense,” Gov. Brown said. “I am pleased to be able to appoint a successor with such impeccable professional credentials.”

Atkins recently retired from the staff of U.S. Sen. Jeff Merkley, where she served as State Director since 2009. Previously, she was Chief of Staff to the Oregon Speaker of the House of Representatives, and, before that, manager of the Women’s and Reproductive Health Section of the Oregon Department of Human Services Office of Family Health. Atkins has also served as Staff Director in the Oregon Senate Majority Office and worked in public affairs and policy analysis for nonprofit organizations such as the United Way of the Columbia/Willamette, Planned Parenthood of the Columbia/Willamette, and the Women’s Equity Action League in Washington, D.C.

View biographical information (PDF) about Jeanne Atkins: https://www.oregon.gov/gov/media/Documents/Jeanne_Atkins_Bio.pdf

headline: Remarks by Oregon Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins
content:
Thank you, Governor Brown, for giving me this tremendous opportunity to serve the people of Oregon. Thank you for your confidence in me, and thank you for your support.

The values, the commitment and the enthusiasm you demonstrated during your years as Secretary of State are an inspiration for me. And I will be diligent in carrying out the responsibilities of this unique public office.

Thanks to all of you for being here to share this shining moment with me — my friends, my colleagues, and some of my family members as well. I’d like to introduce:

My husband, John. Three days ago, on the most beautiful March Sunday ever, we drove to Cannon Beach to remember our honeymoon there and commemorate our 43rd wedding anniversary. I would not be here without his partnership over these years.

My son, also John, who many of you know from his years spent in the Capitol and across the street at the Department of Consumer and Business Services, flew in from Texas to use his last-ever spring break in his final semester of law school to be with his mother on her big day.

My sister, Terry Anne, who interrupted school obligations to drive up from Nevada City, California.

We are all thinking of my late parents today — Roger and Anne Paquette. Both of them had long careers as teachers and as active citizens of their community. They both cared deeply about community, justice, an inclusive democracy and public service. We miss them both.

I was raised in our neighbor state to the north, but I have long been an Oregonian. My mother was born in Portland and my parents met at Linfield College. My sister and I enjoyed Cannon Beach vacations back when it was just a few cabins on the Ecola Creek estuary.

John and I moved to Oregon in 1973, a year after we were married. We lived in Corvallis for seven years, and then an opportunity for John to serve on former Congressman Les AuCoin’s staff took us to Washington, D.C., where I had my first professional job as staff attorney for the Women’s Equity Action League.

We enjoyed our years in Washington, but after the birth of our son a few years later, we came back to Oregon because this is where we wanted to be and where we wanted John to grow up.

John and I have both had great opportunities those 30 years since to make contributions through our work – in Washington County where we live, here in Salem and in towns as varied as Portland, West Linn and Molalla.

I can truthfully say that working in public health for many years, and as State Director for Senator Merkley over the past five years, I have visited every county in Oregon, many more than once, and I appreciate all their historical and cultural uniqueness.

Now I have been given the opportunity to protect and strengthen some of our deepest values as Oregonians:
•Free, open and fair elections.
•Openness and transparency in government.
•Public access to state government records.
•The safeguarding of our historical and official state documents.
•Helping Oregon small businesses grow and thrive by making it easier to start and register a business in Oregon.
•Accountability for our state agencies and offices.

It is my great privilege to be your Secretary of State. As a separate and distinct constitutional officer, the work of the Secretary of State is of fundamental importance to the people of Oregon.

I am grateful to my predecessors in this office, including Governor Brown, for setting the agency on a path for success.

But there is vital work to be done over the next two years, and now as Oregon’s new Secretary of State, I look forward to bringing my ideas, experience and focus – in partnership with legislative leadership, other statewide leaders and the leadership and staff of the Secretary of State’s office – to move that work forward.

And in the time available to me in this special and distinct office, I intend to work every day to improve our government and build public trust.

– Oregon Secretary of State
content2:
Jeanne P. Atkins,
 March 11, 2015

headline: 2015-2016 Oregon Blue Book Available
content:
The 2015-2016 Oregon Blue Book is now available. Copies can be purchased at the State Capitol Gift Shop, from Oregon bookstores or online: https://secure.sos.state.or.us/prs/shopBlueBook.do

Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins will sign copies of the new Blue Book, along with back cover photographer Kenneth Thompson of Tigard, on Tuesday, March 17. Secretary Atkins will sign copies from 11:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. outside the State Capitol Gift Shop in Salem. Front cover photographer Ted Demetriades of Grants Pass is unable to attend the signing.

The new edition of the Blue Book features a cover photo of Lithia Park in Jackson County, which is one of 20 Oregon counties that have never appeared on the cover. The back cover is a photo of a poplar tree farm in Morrow County.

The 2015-16 Blue Book contains winning essays by Oregon schoolchildren in a contest sponsored by the State Archives. Students were asked to describe why they like living in Oregon and their favorite places to visit. The essays will serve as a time capsule for future generations of Oregonians. Essay contest winners include Cierra Shapley from Candy Lane Elementary School in Milwaukie and Inessa Garrey of Judson Middle School in Salem.
https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/explore/exhibits/essays-2015/introduction.aspx

Winners and those who earned honorable mentions will be in Salem on March 17 to sign Blue Books and be recognized on the floors of the Oregon House and Senate.

We also asked all state agencies to look at their entries and rewrite them with a focus on their mission and core business functions. The Oregon economy and government finance sections were rewritten to use plain language to explain complicated subjects. New graphics are included. Also updated: the timeline of Oregon's history to reflect some important dates that were previously not included such as the adoption of Oregon's public accommodation law in 1953 and the Oregon Equality Act in 2007.

The color insert in the 2015-2016 Blue Book explores Oregon's early rural schools. https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/explore/exhibits/education-home.aspx
The feature, which also is viewable online, uses photos, artwork and memorabilia to highlight the colorful history of education in Oregon before World War II.

The Oregon Blue Book has been published every other year since 1911. It contains up-to-date listings and descriptions of government agencies and educational institutions. It includes an almanac, maps and facts about Oregon history and elections, as well as information about the arts, media and cultural institutions in the Beaver State. The Blue Book provides a concise analysis of the state economy, government financing and education strategy. Copies will be distributed free to public schools, community colleges, public universities, public libraries, judges, elected officials, cities, counties, state agencies, boards and commissions, public ports and Oregon's consuls.

The paperback edition sells for $18. The State Capitol Gift Shop will charge $15 for copies purchased on March 17.

headline: Audit: Oregon IT Projects Need More Oversight
content:
The State of Oregon has taken positive steps to ensure the success of major information technology (IT) projects, but further action is needed, according to a new state audit.

The Department of Administrative Services (DAS) should build upon its new model for approving and overseeing major information technology projects. Auditors warned that the model in its current form may not detect or prevent problems such as those the state encountered with its major projects over the last several years.

“Replacing computer systems is difficult and risky. We wanted to see whether the state was taking the right steps to oversee these projects. They are on the right track, but there is more to be done to improve Oregon’s IT project success rate,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins.

Auditors examined reports and documentation about projects over the past ten years to identify common problems and their causes. They interviewed agency managers, many of whom said they lacked the capacity, expertise and experience to plan for and manage replacement of their major IT systems.

The new audit evaluated “Stage Gate Oversight,” a new DAS-run model for overseeing large IT projects. Although the audit concluded that Stage Gate is a step in the right direction, it is not yet fully developed and DAS lacks sufficient staff to fully implement it.

The audit recommends that DAS fully develop and implement Stage Gate and also assign appropriate and sufficient staff to carry out the effort.  DAS should also provide guidance and direction to state agencies that lack resources for developing IT systems and establish formal consequences for failing to meet Stage Gate requirements.

The audit also recommends that DAS formally define the consequences for state agencies that fail to follow Stage Gate requirements.

Many state agencies are operating on older systems that will need to be replaced. Approximately $1 billion of projects are currently underway or in the planning process. Some agencies lack the experience and expertise to manage large IT system development projects and centralized help is not always available.

The audit team consisted of William Garber, Neal Weatherspoon, Erika Ungern, Matthew Owens and Amy Mettler

Read the audit on the Oregon Secretary of State’s website: http://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/recent.aspx

headline: Education Audits Capstone Report: Achieving Oregon’s Education Goals for All Students
content:
Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins has issued a report highlighting recommendations to improve the quality of education.
http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3978102

“High-quality, affordable education is vital to the welfare of all Oregonians,” Secretary Atkins said. “As our economy continues to recover, it is even more important to ensure all students are successful and have the opportunity to fulfill their potential.”

Areas that need continued focus by state agencies, their partners and the Legislature include:
•Coordination and collaboration among agencies and oversight board
•Data-informed instruction and decision-making
•Targeted support services for teachers and students
•The high cost of childcare, which restricts learning
•Education funding to support education goals

State policymakers have set the goal of creating a seamless education system, yet a series of audits conducted by the Secretary of State's office established the need for additional coordination and collaboration among educational institutions, state agencies and other partners. Efforts are under way to develop a shared longitudinal data system, but continued commitment to completing such a system is vital for evaluating student outcomes, including achievement gaps and program efforts and results.

Prior audits found that targeted services to students and support for teachers are essential to ensuring the success of all students. Support such as mentoring for new teachers helps better prepare teachers for the classroom. And mentoring and tutoring services for adult GED students can help break down barriers and improve student success.

Another audit pointed out how the high cost of child care undermines state efforts to provide equitable education. State and federally funded programs that once provided childcare have had to eliminate or reduce this service. Oregon income tax credits for child care are schedule to sunset at the end of 2015.

The recession further limited education funding to match Oregon's goals, particularly for community colleges and universities. The high cost of higher education continues to rise while financial aid has failed to keep pace, putting an advanced degree further out of reach for many Oregonians.

“Our audits point out areas for continued focus by state agencies, oversight boards and the Legislature to ensure we keep improving our education system,” said Gary Blackmer, Audits Division director Gary.

The Education Audits Capstone report was prepared by Sheronne Blasi.

Read the audit on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/stateaudits.aspx

headline: Audit: Cash Flow Problems Threaten Financial Health of Fish and Wildlife Agency
content:
The Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife (ODFW) faces an ongoing cash flow problem that has repeatedly put the agency's financial health at risk, according to a new audit by the Secretary of State's office.
http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3978103

Inflation, expanding responsibilities and a growing maintenance backlog are driving up costs. Meanwhile, revenue is not keeping pace.

“Oregon's rich variety of fish and wildlife is critical to our state's heritage and economy,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “We must look at revenues, expenditures and management practices so that we can continue to protect habitat and provide recreational opportunities for hunting, angling and wildlife viewing.”

Hunting, fishing and wildlife-viewing have a significant economic impact in Oregon. The 2006 National Survey of Fishing, Hunting, and Wildlife-Associated Recreation found that hunters, anglers and wildlife viewers contributed an estimated $2 billion to Oregon's economy using 2013 dollars.

Estimated Economic Impact to Oregon, in 2013 dollars

Hunting …………… $432 million

Angling ……………. $574 million

Wildlife viewing … $897 million

Oregon's commercial fishing industry added at least $618 million to the economy in 2013.

The cost of running the agency has grown faster and has led to repeated and ever-larger budget shortfalls. ODFW relies heavily on license fees to fund its broad range of fish, wildlife and habitat activities. Fee increases have in part covered the shortfalls. Yet, fewer Oregonians are buying hunting and fishing licenses, a decline that began in the 1970s. The audit points out that this business model may not be sustainable in the future.

ODFW has multiple proposals under consideration to address the 2015-17 anticipated budget shortfall including more fee increases, operational efficiencies, program reductions and a request for more general fund support.

“While this report identified a recurring cash flow problem, our work does not end here,” said Gary Blackmer, director of the Oregon Audits Division. “We are beginning a second audit to look further into ODFW's activities and explore strategies for how the agency can manage their breadth of responsibilities and resources.”

Read the audit on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/stateaudits.aspx

Listen to an audio statement by Secretary Atkins on the agency YouTube channel: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z4525DlUfq4

headline: Report on Challenges Facing Oregon
content:
Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins has released a report focusing on three key sectors of state government that pose critical challenges to Oregon’s progress.

“Oregonians need our state to have strong education, infrastructure and finance systems, so we can continue to make economic progress,” Secretary Atkins said.

The new report was informed by multiple audits conducted by the Secretary of State’s office. Secretary Atkins, who took office in March, looked to auditors to put a fresh set of eyes on several years’ worth of auditing to  find common themes to help lawmakers and government leaders move Oregon toward greater prosperity.

The three areas are:
•Educating Oregon’s Workforce: Oregon must have an education system organized for student success and ensure that young Oregonians are prepared to enter the workforce. Audits have made multiple recommendations on how Oregon might better educate and train its workforce.
•Developing Oregon’s Infrastructure: Oregon faces critical infrastructure needs, including the need to upgrade state information technology systems and to develop stable mechanisms for funding and managing future transportation projects. Audits have identified strategies to address these critical infrastructure needs.
•Strengthening Oregon’s Finances: Governments in Oregon face challenging financing issues. Audits have highlighted the financial strain faced by struggling counties and recommended ways to collect more debt owed to the state and to build sustainable finances for state government.

The report highlights these challenges because they affect important fundamental roles of state government: workforce education, infrastructure, and finances. The challenges are large and difficult. While audits have found some progress and small successes, these critical government sectors need more attention and committed efforts from state leaders.

At this point in the legislative session, we want to remind state leaders of the work that has been done to identify where priority action is needed. More challenges exist and future audits will make additional recommendations to help agencies address those challenges.

Read the report: http://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/recent.aspx

Listen to an audio statement: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JpOiV4-qaH0&feature=youtu.be

headline: Audit Wins National Award
content:
The Oregon Audits Division has won a national award for its 2014 report on the state's Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF) program.

Oregon won first place in the National State Auditors Association Excellence in Accountability Awards for large performance audits.

“Every day, the Audits Division is looking for ways to help state agencies better deliver services to Oregonians,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “I'm extremely proud of their work and pleased with this recognition of their excellence.”

Read the audit on the Secretary of State's website: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3520086

TANF sends monthly cash payments to thousands of the poorest families in the state. In return, TANF requires clients to find work or participate in activities to help them become more self sufficient. The audit found little to no progress in moving many clients toward self sufficiency, in part because of deep cuts to client services during the recession. It recommended that DHS significantly raise expectations of clients, build stronger partnerships with Coordinated Care Organizations and other groups to improve client services, and use available data to better assist clients, track their progress, and evaluate the performance of program initiatives.

Auditors visited or called numerous branch offices across the state, interviewing case workers, branch and district managers, and JOBS contractors. They also analyzed more than one million case records; reviewed 81 individual TANF case files; compared Oregon results to national; analyzed TANF studies, economic studies and GAO reports; and evaluated other government agencies that could help DHS meets its program needs.

At the governor's request, DHS is redesigning the program, with an emphasis on an “accountable, flexible, and person-centered approach.” The Governor's Budget for 2015-17 calls for shifting $30 million in savings from caseload reduction to help with DHS's proposed redesign of TANF to emphasize an “accountable, flexible, and person-centered approach.” In January, the Oregon Business Council's annual leadership summit, a gathering of the state's top business leaders, supported the redesign effort, noting that it “responds to a 2014 audit of the program.”

DHS has also asked the Legislature for separate funding to help it gather data from other agencies to improve case management, as the audit recommended.

The audit team consisted of William K. Garber, Sandra K. Hilton, Jamie N. Ralls, Ian M. Green, Scott A. Learn and Hayley A. Pickus.

headline: Statewide Financial Condition Report
content:
Health care costs, violent crime and unemployment insurance are all trending favorably as Oregon's economy continues to recover, according to a [Statewide Financial Condition Report](http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3978109) released today.

“Oregon was hard hit by the Great Recession, but the recovery has seen significant improvement to the state's financial footings,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “Still, there are areas that need attention, including debt payments, programs for vulnerable Oregonians and the state's depleted Rainy Day Fund.”

The recession forced Oregon leaders to make difficult budget decisions to bring spending in line with revenues. The state's biggest challenge was meeting the increased need for state services without enough state revenues.

Since then, some encouraging longer-term trends have emerged:
•State health care costs for employees have leveled off in the past four years, and declined slightly in 2014 as a result of employee contributions.
•PERS assets reached 97% of liabilities, mainly as a result of investment growth.
•Violent crime rates crimes continue their long decline, although property crimes recently increased slightly.
•With federal assistance, Oregon had sufficient unemployment insurance reserves to provide benefits through the recession, and has already made great progress rebuilding the reserves.

Despite the strides, aspects of the state's financial health still need attention:
•The state borrowed to build infrastructure and create jobs but the debt payments continue after the money is spent, leaving less money for future maintenance.
•Spending on health and human services grows year after year, reflecting the continued needs of the Oregonians whose income hasn't rebounded enough from the recovery.
•Oregon relied on its Rainy Day Fund during the recession, but has not yet begun to rebuild it.

“A sound financial condition is vital for sustainable government services,” Atkins said. “We need to continue rebuilding so we can be prepared for the next economic storm.”

Listen to an audio statement here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f3e95RgeBrA&feature=youtu.be

Read the report here: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3978109

headline: Waste, Fraud and Abuse: Annual Report
content:
The Oregon Audits Division today released its annual report on the Government Waste Hotline.

The hotline (800-336-8218) received 180 reports of waste, fraud and abuse in 2014, up about 17% from the year before.

Reports involved allegations of fraud, theft, unethical or improper workplace conduct, time theft and misuse of state vehicles. Reports also were determined to be unrelated to state funds or concerned questions about state policies and procedures.

The Government Waste Hotline was established in 1995 for public employees and members of the public to report waste, inefficiency or abuse by state agencies, state employees or persons under contract with state agencies. In addition to a toll-free telephone line, hotline reports may be submitted by email, fax and by calling directly to the Audits Division.

State law provides confidentiality for the identity of any person making a report through the hotline.

The Audits Division is required to notify the Oregon Government Ethics Commission if potential violations of the Oregon ethics law are discovered. Law enforcement must be notified if potential criminal activity is discovered.

State law requires the Audits Division to conduct an initial investigation of each report of waste, inefficiency or abuse and determine which reports warrant further investigation.

Since the hotline's inception, the Audits Division has estimated about $16 million in questioned costs.

Tips are the most common fraud detection method, according to the Association of Certified Fraud Examiners' 2014 Global Fraud Study.

A copy of the report is available here: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3978110

Listen to an audio statement here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZTGpCMz5A3M&feature=youtu.be

The Audit Team included V. Dale Bond, Sandra K. Hilton, Jamie N. Ralls, Karen M. Peterson, Alan Bell, Melaney Scott, Olivia Recheked and Wendy Kam.

headline: Audit: Target Investments to Improve Community College Completion Rates
content:
State higher education agencies and community colleges should target investments to help community college students complete degrees and certificates, a new audit by the Secretary of State's office found.

The auditors analyzed a cohort of community college students and found that only 24% finished a degree or certificate at an Oregon community college within seven years. The completion rates were even lower for most students of color.

“Community colleges provide access to higher education for some of Oregon's historically underserved students,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “Ensuring that all community college students have the opportunity to succeed is imperative for improving Oregon's economy, addressing income inequality and meeting the state's education and workforce goals.”

Oregon community colleges are making efforts to promote student success by implementing national leading practices. However, most of their strategies reach few students. Capacity constraints at the colleges and at the state limit the impact of these efforts, the audit found.

Oregon is far from reaching its goal that 40% of the adult population will have an associate's degree or post-secondary credential by 2025. Associate's degrees and certificates can confer economic benefits on students as well as provide the skilled employees that industry leaders need to be competitive. Improving student success and completion rates at community colleges could help Oregon achieve this goal.

The audit recommends that the Higher Education Coordinating Commission and the Department of Community Colleges and Workforce Development focus on:
•Increasing capacity to coordinate and support student success efforts
•Improving data analysis capacity, support, and infrastructure
•Monitoring the impact of new proposals

“This report highlights the need for continued analysis and follow through to ensure that students realize the benefits of new programs and initiatives,” said Gary Blackmer, director of the Oregon Audits Division.

Read the audit on the Secretary of State website: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3978111

headline: Oregon Registers 500th Benefit Company
content:
Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins announced today that 500 Oregon businesses have registered as Benefit Companies under a new designation that became available less than 18 months ago.

The number of businesses registering as Benefit Companies grows every week and includes companies from dozens of communities across Oregon.

http://sos.oregon.gov/business/Pages/benefit-company.aspx

An up-to-date list is available on the Oregon Secretary of State’s website: http://sos.oregon.gov/business/Pages/oregon-benefit-companies.aspx

“Oregon is a hotbed of socially conscious entrepreneurs looking for a new way conduct business,” said Secretary Atkins. “It’s very exciting to see the number of Benefit Companies grow so rapidly.”

Living Room Realty owner and founder Jenelle Isaacson praised the new designation.

“Living Room Realty wanted to become a Benefit Company because of our belief that we build a business by building community one great neighbor at a time. It was an extension of our company’s social, environmental and ethical values,” said Isaacson. “The designation has been a way to recognize the hard work of our staff who are dedicated to doing business with heart and meaning not just for a paycheck.”

House Bill 2296, which established a new way for businesses to organize to help create high quality jobs, went into effect Jan. 1, 2014. It was approved by a bipartisan majority of the 2013 Oregon Legislature. Under Oregon’s law, Benefit Companies enjoy legal protection to create value for society, not just shareholders, while meeting higher standards of accountability and transparency.

A Benefit Company is a new kind of business legally required to: 1) create a materially positive impact on society and the environment; 2) expand its fiduciary duties to require consideration of the interests of workers, community and the environment; and 3) publicly report annually on its overall social and environmental performance using comprehensive, credible, independent and transparent third party standards. B Company status offers businesses more freedom to define success and provides legal protection for those looking to pursue a corporate purpose other than exclusively maximizing profits for shareholders.

Listen to an audio statement from Secretary Atkins here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GLpkn62fxi8&feature=youtu.be

headline: Audits Division Wins Second National Award
content:
A state audit that recommended ways to improve access to Oregon's GED programs has received a national award.

The National Legislative Program Evaluation Society (NLPES) recognized the 2013 GED audit for addressing the 11% of Oregonians without a high school diploma or equivalent. The GED audit received an NLPES Impact Award for bringing the issue to the attention of state leadership and community groups. As a result, the Governor's Budget directed additional resources to GED programs in community colleges.

“This is well-deserved recognition for a top-notch State Audits Division,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “Every day, they are looking for ways to better deliver critical services to Oregonians.”

The audit recommended better coordination by state agencies and local community colleges. In addition, continued collaboration is needed between high schools, community colleges, and other agencies and organizations to help ensure clients needing a GED are referred to local programs.

It's the second time in the last month that the Oregon Audits Division has received national recognition. In May, Oregon won first place in the National State Auditors Association Excellence in Accountability Awards for large performance audits for its 2014 report on the state's Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF) program.

“We are fortunate in Oregon to have leadership that recognizes the value of performance auditing and puts our recommendations into action,” said Gary Blackmer, Audits Division Director. “We are all striving to provide better services for Oregonians.”

Read the GED audit here: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/2530704

Read the TANF audit here: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/3520086

Listen to an audio statement here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m1bJu0uBIW8&feature=youtu.be

The GED audit team consisted of Sheronne Blasi and Nicole Pexton.

headline: Incubators, Makerspaces and Accelerators: Helping Oregon Entrepreneurs Thrive
content:
In celebration of the national “Week of Making,” (https://www.whitehouse.gov/nation-of-makers) Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins today released a list of small business incubators, accelerators and makerspaces in Oregon.

“Nurturing entrepreneurs and small businesses is critical to Oregon’s economy,” said Secretary Atkins. “I want every Oregonian with a great idea to get the support they need to succeed.”

The National Maker Faire, held from June 12 to June 18 in Washington, D.C., brings together government agencies and “makers” from across the country. The event promotes inventors, designers, students and makers who develop creative solutions and bring innovations to market.

Maker spaces foster small-batch manufacturing by providing training and equipment.

Incubators help entrepreneurs get access to peer and professional counseling that helps them launch their business ideas. Accelerators connect small business owners with the capital to grow.

The list of 44 Oregon incubators, makerspaces and accelerators includes organizations across the state. It was compiled by the Office of Small Business Assistance, which is housed in the Corporation Division of the Secretary of State’s office.
https://data.oregon.gov/Business/Maker-Spaces-Business-Incubators-Accelerators/wpin-z8u6


The White House celebrates a “Week of Making” from June 12 to 18. The goals include creating hands-on learning opportunities for students to engage in STEM arts and design; broadening participation for girls, young women and underrepresented minorities; supporting the development of low-cost tools for making; developing capabilities to help “maker entrepreneurs” produce products domestically; and engaging makers to develop solutions for pressing local and global challenges.

Listen to an audio statement by Secretary Atkins here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GDPoWiqEw_g&feature=youtu.be

headline: Limited Number of Oregon Blue Books Are Available
content:
A limited number of the newly-released 2015-16 Oregon Blue Book are still available. This edition features an expanded color section exploring Oregon’s early rural schools with photos, artwork and memorabilia from Oregon’s historical societies. The purpose of the exhibit is to look back at the colorful history of rural education in Oregon before World War II when small town life revolved around the local schoolhouse.

The 2015-16 Blue Book also features — for the first time — winning essays from a statewide contest that asked elementary and middle school children what they loved about Oregon. These essays provide a fresh perspective on our state and create a time capsule for Oregonians in the future.

The Oregon Blue Book, Oregon’s official fact book and almanac, has been updated and re-published every two years since 1911. It is an institution, a state icon and a collector’s item. This year’s Blue Book had a limited run and only about 1,300 remain. They can be purchased from the State Archives online: https://secure.sos.state.or.us/prs/shopBlueBook.do
Or at the Capitol Gift Shop, Powell’s Portland locations and a limited number of bookstores around the state.

If you would like more information about the Blue Book and its availability you can contact Julie Yamaka at 503-378-5199 or julie.a.yamaka@state.or.us

Listen to an audio statement by Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PJIwyYn10eM

headline: Independent Party of Oregon Qualifies as Major Political Party
content:
Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins today announced that the Independent Party of Oregon has qualified to be a major political party and will be permitted to participate in the May 2016 Primary Election.

In order to qualify as a major political party in Oregon, a party must have 108,739 registered voters 275 days before the 2016 May Primary. The Independent Party of Oregon had 109,363 registered voters as of Aug. 16, the deadline.

Minor Political parties will be able to participate in the November 2016 General Election by nominating candidates after the May Primary. 
http://sos.oregon.gov/voting/Pages/voteinor.aspx

headline: Audit Finds Weaknesses in State Data Center
content:
Only recently have the Governor, Legislature and state Chief Information Officer taken the first steps to address security weaknesses at the state's data center that have persisted for nine years, according to a Secretary of State audit released today. http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/4119389

The audit reviewed security of the data center operated by the Department of Administrative Services' Enterprise Technology Division. State agencies use the data center's complex and extensive inventory of computers and networks to run hundreds of their programs. A breach in state systems could result in significant loss of sensitive data about Oregonians, such as tax or medical records and social security numbers that could be used in fraud or identity theft.

The report identifies six critical security problems that have never been resolved, although auditors issued warnings dating back as far as 2006. The problems include inadequate management of system configurations, insufficient monitoring of networks and users with special system access, inadequate incident tracking, and obsolete hardware and software. Collectively, the problems heighten the risk to computer programs and information at the data center.

Auditors concluded that the state has been unable to improve security because management abandoned initial data center security plans, did not assign security roles and responsibilities, or provide sufficient security staff. As a result, efforts to improve security often ended in partially implemented solutions. Even if alerts sounded, in many cases no one had the authority or responsibility to resolve them.

“Oregon must do more to protect its data systems,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “The risks identified in this audit make it clear the urgency we face.”

The report noted that organizational changes to improve security occurred in the last six months. The state Chief Information Officer now answers directly to the Governor.  In addition, the 2015 Legislature formalized the state Chief Information Officer's responsibility for information technology throughout all state agencies, including the data center. This also brought the data center under the direct responsibility of the Chief Information Officer. These changes heightened attention on security and managers are now starting to build the security function into the data center as originally planned.

Auditors stated that the organizational changes were appropriate and necessary but also indicated that resolving the many longstanding security weaknesses will require significant resources, time and perseverance, along with the cooperation of other state agencies. Auditors noted that they will be starting an audit of security issues in agency computer programs, and also return to the data center in two years to report on its progress.

Auditors also recognized management for the unique agreement with the state of Montana to quickly restore operations after a serious disaster or disruption by copying its systems and records to Montana's State Data Center. This approach could assist data center recovery but auditors noted additional work remained to replicate some systems and fully test the plans.

Read the audit here: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/stateaudits.aspx
The audit team consisted of William Garber, Neal Weatherspoon, Teresa Furnish and Amy Mettler.

headline: Audit Finds Ambiguity in Lottery’s Casino Enforcement
content:
When is a casino not a casino?  In Oregon, it's hard to tell.

Video gambling machines are a major source of income for a number of retailers even though the Oregon Constitution prohibits “casinos.”  Trouble is, casinos are not defined in Oregon law, with the result that the prohibition is not currently subject to effective enforcement.

These are among the findings of an audit of the Oregon State Lottery conducted by the Oregon Secretary of State's Audits Division. http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/4119420

“It's clear that neither the Constitution nor the Legislature has provided clarification as to what constitutes a casino,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “Still, the Lottery must examine its own policies and procedures to make sure that it is not licensing illegal casinos.”

Casinos operated by recognized Indian tribes on reservation lands are exempt from the Oregon prohibition on casinos.

Auditors examining a selection of establishments known as “Limited Menu Retailers” found that some are earning more than 50% of their income from gambling machines such as video poker, in violation of the income rule established by the Lottery Commission.

The income rule has been revised by the Commission from time to time, in an effort to follow a 1997 Oregon Supreme Court ruling that businesses should not feature gambling as a “dominant purpose” or “dominant use”. Like the word “casino,” these terms have not been defined by the courts or the Oregon Legislature.

At the same time, the Commission has altered its investigatory standards. Lottery inspectors are currently relying on a visual inspection to decide whether a retail establishment “looks” like a casino rather than comparing their sales income and lottery income.

About 230 of the 2,300 video machine operators fall into Lottery's “Limited Menu Retailers” category, operating 11% of the video gambling machines in Oregon. These establishments generated about 21% of the Lottery's total net receipts from all machines. Last year they received about $34 million in commissions and generated about $125 million in state revenues. Nine individuals or business entities own nearly half of these Limited Menu Retailers.

The audit also found that some retailers are giving away food and beverages to customers, but reporting them as sales, which results in a bottom line that looks less dependent on gambling revenues than it actually is.

Auditors looked at this category of small restaurants and how Lottery was enforcing the casino prohibition. Most of the businesses reviewed were earning more than 50% of their income from the machines.

Oregon Lottery rules limit the number of gaming machines a retailer may have. The retailer earns a commission for each machine. The average commission for a single machine was $26,111 last year.

Auditors recommend that the Lottery Commission seek a clear and enforceable definition of “casino” by working with the Lottery Commission, the Governor, and the Legislature. They also recommend that Lottery verify gross sales reports that retailers submit. For retailers earning more than 50% of their income from video gambling machines, Lottery should evaluate whether removing a machine would enable the retailer to comply with the Oregon Constitution's restriction against operating as a “casino.”

You can read the audit here: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/stateaudits.aspx

The audit team consisted of Mary Wenger, Dale Bond, Alan Bell and Joseph Flager.

headline: Lack of Party Affiliation Not a Bar to Appearing on Presidential Primary Ballot
content:
Oregon Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins announced today that Oregon law does not require candidates for President of the United States to be declared members of the political party for which they seek to be nominated.  Under Oregon law, a candidate for President can be listed on the primary ballot if the Secretary of State determines that the person’s candidacy is “generally advocated or is recognized in national news media,” or if the candidate obtains sufficient signatures on a nominating petition from voters throughout Oregon.

As in past presidential elections, Sec. Atkins will announce a preliminary list of candidates who meet the requirement of being generally advocated or recognized in the national media. The preliminary list will be announced Tuesday, Jan. 5, 2016.

The announcement today is in response to inquiries by the press and others regarding the candidacy of Sen. Bernie Sanders, who is not a registered member of a political party in his home state of Vermont but who is seeking the Democratic presidential nomination.

headline: Oregon Motor Voter Update
content:
Dear fellow Oregonians:

Less than six months ago, Gov. Brown signed the Oregon Motor Voter Act (HB 2177) into law.  Since the law was enacted, my team at the Secretary of State’s office has made great progress – in partnership with the Driver and Motor Vehicle Services Division (DMV) — toward implementing Oregon’s innovative new law aimed at putting a ballot in the hand of virtually every eligible Oregonian.  I wanted to take a moment to update you all on our progress, and to let you know of the next steps we are taking toward implementing this groundbreaking new law.

The first goal is to meet HB 2177’s target implementation date for Oregon Motor Voter of January 1, 2016.  From that day forward Oregon citizens visiting the DMV will have the benefit of registering to vote through the new Oregon Motor Voter process. Data from those DMV visits will be provided to the Secretary of State’s Office so it can be matched against our statewide voter registration list. Citizens who are not registered to vote will then be sent a mailing giving them 21 days to “opt-out.” Nobody will be registered to vote before getting this opportunity to decline voter registration.  If they choose to opt out they will not be registered voters. The mailing will also give them the chance to make a party membership choice if they wish.

Later in the year we plan to evaluate past DMV encounter data and will similarly contact those individuals who demonstrated they were qualified to vote but who are not registered.  We believe that getting the program up and running is important to accomplish before evaluating past DMV data. We also think that experience will give us important information about how best to ensure our communications with Oregonians make it easy and clear for them to make their choices under the program.

To meet the January 1, 2016, implementation target date, my team at the Oregon Secretary of State’s office has already met two key milestones, and we are about to embark on a third.

First, we have held successful Rule Advisory Committee meetings. On July 6th and 7th, we held two Rule Advisory Committee meetings at the Oregon State Capitol.  We invited a broad spectrum of stakeholders and interested parties, including all major and minor political parties, domestic violence and confidentiality advocates, voter engagement groups, and others to attend the meetings. I found the meetings to be very helpful, and I wanted to thank the Legislators from both political parties who encouraged us to use a Rule Advisory Committee. It was a very useful suggestion and I wanted to thank you for this sound advice.

Second, the draft rule outlining how the program will work has been published for comment. The August 1st Oregon Bulletin published the draft Oregon Administrative Rule implementing Oregon Motor Voter, and a link to the draft rule is available here: http://sos.oregon.gov/voting/Pages/motor-voter.aspx.  The proposed rule is in the form of a manual, similar to our Vote-By-Mail Manual and Campaign Finance Manual. We find these manuals to be more accessible and understandable to the general public, as well as the state and county staff who will help implement the law. Comments on the draft rule are due by September 25, 2015, and can be submitted by email to elections.sos@state.or.us. We welcome your thoughts.

Third, we will hold a series of public hearings in September. As part of our rulemaking, I feel it is vitally important to get feedback from citizens throughout Oregon on this new voter registration process. To seek that feedback, we have scheduled four public hearings throughout Oregon as follows: September 1st in Medford, September 3rd in Portland, September 9th in Pendleton, and September 24th in Eugene. For specific hearing times and locations, please visit our Public Hearing website (http://sos.oregon.gov/voting/Pages/motor-voter.aspx).  You are all welcome to attend these hearings, and please feel free to pass this informatio
content2:
n on to your constituents. We want to hear from all of you.

Finally, just last week I attended the annual Association of County Clerks Conference, and I wanted to express my thanks to every member of the Legislature for your help ensuring that the counties receive the necessary financial support to implement this new law. The county clerks are our partners in this endeavor, and they are responsible for doing the important, daily work of processing new voter registrations. Our county partners are already engaged in the necessary preparatory work to ensure a successful implementation of the new law starting on January 1, 2016, and I appreciate the financial support offered by the Legislature to help make our effort a success both at the state and local level.

Thank you again to all of you for your thoughtful and candid advice regarding the implementation of Oregon Motor Voter. I value all the different perspectives regarding this new voter registration process, and have worked to respond to the concerns raised during the debate in constructing the program. What makes Oregon special today is the same thing that has always made Oregon special – our ability to agree wherever possible, to disagree respectfully when necessary, and to always move forward together in the public’s interest.  As I enter my sixth month in office as your Secretary of State, I want to thank you all as we move forward together to implement Oregon Motor Voter and help put a ballot in the hand of virtually every eligible Oregonian.
 

Sincerely,
Jeanne P. Atkins
Oregon Secretary of State

headline: More Can Be Done to Improve Treatment at State Hospital
content:
The Oregon State Hospital has made progress during a decade of change, but more can be done to improve mental health treatment, staff safety and fatigue, and the electronic records system, according to an audit released today by the Secretary of State's office.

The most visible change to the hospital occurred in 2011, when the original structures on hospital grounds were rebuilt or replaced. The new facilities incorporated a centralized treatment model, also known as “treatment malls.” In this model, patients live on their units and attend group therapy classes in a different part of the hospital— similar to how a community college might operate.

“The hospital's adoption of treatment malls is part of Oregon's move to mental healthcare that is recovery-oriented. This is important because people with mental illness can get better,” said Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins. “With this in mind, staff can help patients strive to live a self-directed life and reach their full potential through the recovery process.”

Internal changes have been less visible to the public.

The hospital now offers more group therapy options, and introduced strategies to create a safer environment for staff and patients. Hospital leadership is also working to reduce overtime and implement an electronic health record system.

“Our audit found that, though the hospital made significant improvements, there are additional actions the hospital can take to further improve safety and promote patient recovery,” said Gary Blackmer, director of the Oregon Audits Division.

The auditors found that cultural and organizational changes around safety have taken hold, but challenges remain. The hospital's own data suggests that patient aggression towards peers and staff increased in 2014. Results from a 2014 survey indicate 40% of nursing staff surveyed were assaulted over the last year, and 43% felt unsafe in their jobs.

Improvement in treatment planning and therapy groups could also help patients recover. The auditors found that the hospital provides little guidance and training on conducting case formulations, an important tool clinicians use to identify patients' treatment needs. The hospital could better align treatment planning and delivery by using patient's treatment goals to inform its processes for designing, selecting, scheduling, and evaluating therapy groups offered on the malls.

The audit recommends management continue to address organization culture and training needs. It also recommends the hospital develop and document a plan for improving consistency of case formulations and integrating patient treatment goals with the treatment mall groups offered.

Although the hospital has reduced overtime, concerns remain around how it manages staff fatigue. The audit report recommends the hospital work to limit unscheduled absences, a major driver of overtime. The hospital should also develop strategies to manage the amount of overtime individual staff work, and provide training on the effects of staff fatigue on patient care.

While efforts were made to implement an electronic health records system, parts of the system are still incomplete, adversely affecting efficiency and potentially the quality and cost of patient care. Auditors recommend the hospital complete the electronic health record system implementation, prioritizing automating processes that significantly affect patient care and converting critical patient information to electronic format.

Read the full audit on the Secretary of State website: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/4270919
Or an executive summary on the Audits Division Blog: https://oregonaudits.org/2015/09/09/oregon-state-hospital-significant-actions-taken-to-improve-safety-and-promote-patient-recovery-but-further-improvements-are-possible/

headline: Secretary of State Atkins Celebrates National Voter Registration Day
content:
Today at the Cedar Mills Community Library, Secretary of State Jeanne P. Atkins will remind Oregonians that citizen engagement begins in our local communities.  As part of a National Voter Registration Day celebration at the library, Secretary Atkins will encourage communities throughout Oregon to register to vote.  Believing that local communities are at the heart of citizen engagement in Oregon, Secretary Atkins will share the story of a local community leader, former Linn County Clerk Del Riley, who helped bring Vote-By-Mail to Oregon by making Linn County the first in Oregon to use this innovative system to promote voter participation.

Secretary Atkins will also discuss the next step to promote voter engagement in Oregon:  Oregon Motor Voter.  In a statement released in advance of this evening’s event, Secretary Atkins said, “Vote-By-Mail was new in Oregon and now it’s part of our DNA. I expect the new Oregon Motor Voter program to takes its place alongside Vote-By-Mail as another fundamental and cherished part of our election system.”

“Oregon has long been a pioneer in voter engagement,” said Secretary Atkins.  ”We make it easy to vote through Vote-By-Mail.  And when Governor Kate Brown signed the Oregon Motor Voter Act (HB 2177) into law this year, Oregon once again helped lead the way in removing barriers to registration.  The new Oregon Motor Voter program will help put a ballot in the hands of virtually every eligible Oregonian.”

The Oregon Secretary of State’s office is working to launch this groundbreaking program on January 1, 2016. From that day forward Oregon citizens visiting a DMV office will have the benefit of registering to vote through the new Oregon Motor Voter process.

Oregon Motor Voter provides that data from the DMV visits of Oregonians who meet age, residence, and citizenship requirements will be provided to the Secretary of State’s Office so it can be matched against our statewide voter registration list.   Those who are not yet registered to vote will then be sent a mailing giving them 21 days to “opt-out,” or to add to their registration record by choosing a party affiliation.   Nobody will be registered to vote before getting this opportunity to decline voter registration.  Soon after implementation of the ongoing program, the Secretary of State will work to register eligible voters who created new or updated records with the DMV in the recent past.

Oregon’s pioneering spirit has caught the attention of some members of Congress and other states on the forefront of voting rights. Similar legislation has been introduced in Congress and in more than 15 other states.
http://sos.oregon.gov/voting/Pages/motor-voter.aspx

headline: State Debt Collection Efforts Fall Short, Require Sustained Focus to Improve
content:
Liquidated and delinquent debts owed to the State of Oregon have almost doubled since 2008, to nearly $3.2 billion, and the state needs a sustained focus to improve collections performance over time, a new audit released by Secretary of State Jeanne Atkins found.
http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/4271036

Collection rates on delinquent receivables have dropped since 2008, according to data agencies report to the Legislative Fiscal Office. The audit found that the recession, a lack of sustained focus on improvement, and Oregon's highly decentralized approach to collections likely contributed to debt growth and reduced performance.

“This is our sixth collections-related audit since 1997. Significant improvements identified in those audits have not been implemented, some dating back 18 years,” said Secretary of State Jeanne Atkins “Our state government needs to make delinquent debt collection a consistently high priority.”

Oregon has not implemented some productive collection tools used by other states, has not resolved lingering legal issues that hinder collections, and has allowed inadequate performance measurement to persist.

Some agencies have made collections improvements. However, the audit identified four tools the state has considered for years, but not implemented: vendor offset, expanded levies on debtor bank accounts, a state lien registry, and internet posting of debtors. The audit work on vendor offset identified over 9,000 state debtors on the state vendor list who had received payments from the state or were authorized to receive payments.

The audit included discussions with leading states on debt collection. That work highlighted the importance of having a system “expert” responsible for identifying potential improvements, looking outside the state for new opportunities and reporting to decision makers. The auditors recommended the Department of Administrative Services serve as a statewide strategist on debt collection.

The Audits Division issued an interim report to the Legislature to suggest further legislative changes while the audit was in progress.  At the auditors' recommendation, Senate Bill 55 in the 2015 legislative session charged the Department of Administrative Services with monitoring and improving debt collection. The bill, signed by the governor in July, was initially drafted by the Institute for Modern Government at Willamette University. The Department of Administrative Services also contributed to the bill.

“Collections will not improve overnight, but improvements over time could bring in significant dollars,” said Audits Division Director Gary Blackmer. If Oregon had collected delinquent debt at a 13.5% rate in 2014 – last achieved in 2008 – the state would have brought in nearly $90 million more in collections.

Read this audit and others on the Secretary of State's website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/stateaudits.aspx

headline: Oregon Archives hosting Halloween Open House, Scavenger Hunt
subheadline: Family-friendly event will give Oregonians close up view of state’s historic records, including original Oregon Constitution
content:
In recognition of American Archives Month, the Oregon State Archives will hold a special Halloween version of its annual Archives Open House on Saturday, October 31, from 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM.

The event will include a scavenger hunt/mystery tour that will give the public firsthand experience using the records in the State Archives. Halloween treats and light refreshments will be provided.

In addition to the scavenger hunt/mystery tour, staff will provide visitors with a chance to go “behind the scenes” of State Archives to see the records storage area, the facility’s state-of-the art compact shelving, and learn more about the Archives and its holdings. The 20-30 minute tours will provide an opportunity to see some of Oregon’s most significant historical documents, including the original Oregon Constitution. 

“Our state archives are always accessible and available to the public, but this yearly Open House provides a festive opportunity to learn more about our state’s diverse and fascinating history,” said Secretary of State Jeanne Atkins. “I encourage Oregonians to visit their state archives building and meet some of the highly-skilled archives researchers who work to preserve Oregon’s past.”

The Archives will also be open for visitors to conduct personal research, with staff ready to help the public access archival records. In the gallery, the new exhibit “I Found it in the Archives” will be on display. This exhibit is a collaborative effort by State Archives researchers, volunteers, student interns and staff who have shared their stories of discovery. These stories highlight the diversity of records held at the State Archives, demonstrate the personal value of each research project, and will help encourage research on the state’s one-of-a-kind primary records. 

The State Archives is located as 800 Summer Street NE in Salem. Normal operating hours are 8:00 AM to 4:45 PM Monday through Friday.
http://sos.oregon.gov/archives/Pages/archives-month.aspx

headline: After 30 Years of Public Auditing, Gary Blackmer Announces Retirement
subheadline: Blackmer wraps up a distinguished and respected career as government watchdog
content:
State auditor Gary Blackmer announced his retirement this morning, effective December 31st. He was appointed Director of the Audits Division in 2009 by Kate Brown, then Oregon Secretary of State. Previously, Blackmer served 10 years as the elected Portland City Auditor, eight years as the elected Multnomah County Auditor, as a management auditor in Portland, and as an analyst for a variety of state and local agencies.

“Oregon is better because of Gary’s years of service. Auditors may not be known for their glamorous jobs, but Gary and his team at State Audits are some of the most professional, respected, independent and intelligent people I have ever worked alongside. They serve Oregon well and with distinction,” said Secretary of State Jeanne Atkins.

Recently, the Audits Division won prestigious national awards for a performance audit of Temporary Assistance for Needy Families from the National State Auditors Association, and from the National Legislative Program Evaluation Society for its audit about GED completions in Oregon.

Under Blackmer’s direction the Audits Division has issued over 75 performance audits, 20 information technology audits and management letters, and 110 financial audits. The auditors delve into the workings of government finances, computer systems, and services. They analyze data and case files, examine the logic of computer programs, observe the delivery of services, interview stakeholders and agency personnel, and conduct research about other states’ practices.

“To make government better, we serve as the mechanics that spot problems deep in the machinery of state government,” said Blackmer. “We don’t stop when we find a problem, we also find the path to a solution.”

For the past seven years, Blackmer served as a local and now a state member on the highly selective Government Accountability Office’s Domestic Working Group, comprised of the Comptroller General, six inspectors general, seven state auditors, and six local auditors. Blackmer is a recipient of the 2010 Excellence in Performance and Accountability Award from the National Intergovernmental Audit Forum, and the Lifetime Achievement Award of the Association of Local Government Auditors in 2015. He also served as Chair of the Pacific Northwest Intergovernmental Audit Forum, and President of the Association of Local Government Auditors.

“I’m grateful to our current and former Secretaries of State for supporting our independence and professionalism as auditors, which is critical to our success,” said Blackmer. “I was amazed at the depth of talent in the Audits Division when I arrived six years ago, and it has been exciting to see how their work has brought meaningful improvements for Oregonians. I am confident that they will continue their excellent work under our interim director and Secretary of State.”

Secretary Atkins has appointed Mary Wenger as the Interim Auditor upon Blackmer’s retirement. Wenger previously served as the Deputy Director overseeing financial audits. The appointment of a new audits director will be left open to the next Secretary of State, to be elected in November 2016.

The Audits Division conducts performance, financial, and information technology audits, monitors financial audits of local governments, and responds to hotline allegations.

headline: State Agencies Demonstrate Mixed Results in Public Records Audit
content:
While Oregon agencies follow the public records law for most public records requests, more needs to be done to address the complex, non-routine requests that agencies receive, according to an audit released today by the Secretary of State's office.
http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/4272024

Auditors examined nine state agencies of varying sizes and missions to determine how agencies are both responding to public records requests and retaining their public records. Their findings are outlined in the audit report released today, entitled: “State Agencies Respond Well to Routine Public Records Requests, but Struggle with Complex Requests and Emerging Technologies.”

The audit found that most public records requests agencies receive are simple and can be fulfilled within just a couple weeks, often at little or no cost. But agencies struggle to respond to the occasional complex request, leading at times to delays, high fees, and the perception that agencies are using these tactics to block the release of public information.
