headline: Secretary Clarno Welcomes New Interim Deputy
content:
Today, Secretary of State Bev Clarno announced that Jeff Morgan has been sworn in as Interim Deputy Secretary of State. Jeff has over 39 years of public service in the State of Oregon and has been with the agency as Director of our Business Services Division since 2004. He has also been nationally recognized as an exemplary manager and public servant.  

“Jeff has been a wonderful member of the leadership team as Director of the Business Services Division and I am very pleased that he has agreed to step up to the position of Interim Deputy,” Secretary Clarno said. “I am confident that he will help guide this agency and assist me in managing the important duties of the office.”

Jeff has been happily married to his lovely wife, Patti, for over 44 years. They have 3 children and 2 grandchildren.

headline: What you need to know about Real ID in Oregon and other updates
subheadline: News from the Secretary of State
content:
Real ID
Something big is happening October first of this year. If you aren’t paying attention, it might mean missing your flight out of PDX or any other airport across the U.S.

On that date the Transportation Security Administration, or TSA, begins requiring a new type of identification to board a commercial aircraft. The new ID must be compliant with something called the Real ID Act. It’s designed to keep us safer in the air, but if you try to board an aircraft using your current Oregon driver license starting in October – it won’t work. That’s because the current Oregon driver license is not Real ID compliant.

Oregon DMV will begin offering a Real ID option on July 6, 2020. To fulfill the demand of nearly one million Oregonians who will want the Real ID option, DMV would have to issue 32 licenses a second every business day from July to October. That’s just not possible.

If you don’t have a Real ID compliant form of ID at the airport, TSA will put you through an alternate identity verification process that could take an hour or more, and you could miss your flight.

There is an answer for Oregonians: obtain and use a passport or passport card. The cost of getting a new passport card is roughly equal to that of getting a replacement license with the Real ID option – and you can apply now at one of over 76 acceptance sites across Oregon.

To find out more, visit [https://www.oregon.gov/realid](https://www.oregon.gov/realid).

By the way, Washingtonians already have the option of getting a Real ID compliant driver license. If you are a Washington resident, go to [https://id2020wa.com/](https://id2020wa.com/) to find out more.


Redmond Executive Association
On December 19, I attended and spoke at a meeting of the Redmond Executive Association, a group that was formed to encourage the involvement of Redmond business leaders in civic life by providing a forum for the exchange of ideas and building of relationships. Each month, the Association invites a speaker to discuss issues relating to business, community life, and politics. I provided an overview of the various functions and divisions of the Secretary of State’s office (they were particularly interested in the things that our Corporation Division and Office of Small Business Assistance do to assist our business community), as well as an update on how the 2019 legislative session affected those functions, which I’ve explained in a [prior newsletter.](https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/ORSOS/bulletins/251acf8) I want to thank those who attended for their engagement and leadership in the Redmond community.


Sheriff Nelson
Also while in my hometown of Redmond over the holidays, I ran into Deschutes County Sheriff Shane Nelson and his family at a restaurant. I’ve known Sheriff Nelson for many years, so it was nice to visit with him. One thing I learned was that his son, Austin, recently became an Oregon State Trooper. “Like father, like son,” I thought. In addition, Sheriff Nelson’s wife, Lisa, is an Officer in the Bend Police Department.

This doesn’t have much to do with my service as Secretary of State, but it reminded me of the gratitude we owe our public safety community for the sacrifices that they make on our behalf. Like the rest of us, they have families, friends, and neighbors—and many of them risk those things every single day on the job. National Thank a Police Officer Day isn’t until the third Saturday of September, but that doesn’t mean you have to wait until then to let one know that you appreciate their service!


Kid Governor Inauguration
On January 8, the Third Annual Inauguration Ceremony for Oregon’s Kid Governor took place in the Senate Chamber of the Oregon State Capitol. Oregon’s 3rd Kid Governor is Raaga Mandala, a fifth grader at Jacob Wismer Elementary School in Beaverton. Paul DeMuniz, a former Chief Justice of the Oregon Supreme Court, administered Raaga’s oath of office while Misha Isaak, General Counsel to the Gov
content2:
ernor; James Egan, Chief Judge of the Oregon Court of Appeals; and Courtney Neron, State Representative from House District 26, spoke about the functions of the Executive, Judicial, and Legislative branches of Oregon’s government. The other six finalists in Raaga’s election were also recognized at the ceremony.

Raaga was elected by fifth graders from across the state based on her platform of transitioning homeless individuals to permanent housing. Specifically, her three point plan of “Learn, Love, Legislate” includes learning about the homeless in classrooms; loving the homeless by starting kindness clubs in schools that will collect food and clothing and then give them to homeless shelters; and legislating, where fifth graders will write letters to their legislators, encouraging them to address our housing crisis. You can watch Raaga’s campaign video here: https://youtu.be/C4YJGglPGSQ


Swearing-In Ceremony
As I also explained in [a prior newsletter,](https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/ORSOS/bulletins/251acf8) if a seat in the Legislature becomes vacant with more than two months until the next general election, a new legislator is appointed in a two-step process. First, the former legislator’s political party must nominate between three and five candidates. Then, the Commissioners for the Counties within the district must select one of those nominees. If this process is not completed within thirty days of the vacancy, the Governor can unilaterally appoint someone.

On January 9, I administered the oath of office to newly-appointed State Senator Lynn Findley. In January of 2018, Findley was appointed to fill a vacancy in the Oregon House of Representatives for District 60, which covers almost a quarter of the state’s land area in the far east. That November, he was elected to a full two year term. With the recent resignation of Senator Cliff Bentz, however, Findley has been appointed Senator for Oregon Senate District 30, which covers more than a third of the state, stretching from The Dalles in the northwest to Burns and beyond in the south. Before coming to the Legislature, Senator Findley served as City Manager for the City of Vale, a Justice of the Peace for Harney County, and an employee of the Federal Bureau of Land Management. With his wealth of experience, I know that Senator Findley will continue to serve as an effective voice for rural Oregonians. Welcome, Senator Findley!


Legislative Days
You may already know that Oregon has a part-time Legislature. Under the state constitution, the Legislature is in session for a maximum of 160 calendar days during odd-numbered years, known as “Long Sessions,” and 35 calendar days during even-numbered years, known as “Short Sessions.” It can also convene for “Emergency Sessions.” While the Legislature can’t pass laws in between sessions, it convenes in Salem for four days every eight weeks during the interim to hold informational hearings on topics that could lead to legislation in future sessions, hear updates on past legislation, hear reports from state agencies and Task Forces and, in the Senate, make executive appointments. These are known as "Legislative Days."

In preparation for the upcoming Short Session, which begins on February 3, Legislative Days were held from January 13 to 16—and the Capitol was buzzing with activity all week long. In between Committee and Task Force meetings, I ran into Lake County Commissioner Mark Albertson and Union County Commissioner Paul Anderes, who had just finished testifying on proposed legislation. I appreciate their willingness to make the trip across the state to advocate for their communities.


Lake Oswego Rotary Club
On January 13, I attended a weekly meeting of the Lake Oswego Rotary Club. Like the Redmond Executive Association, the Rotary Club is a group of business and professional leaders who strive to be civically engaged. More specifically, the Rotary Club focuses on providing humanitarian aid, advocat
content3:
ing for professional ethics, and promoting peace around the world. Once again, I provided attendees with an overview of the various functions of the Secretary of State’s office. In particular, I focused on the efforts of our Elections Division to combat voter misinformation ahead of the 2020 Presidential Election, our work with the federal government to ensure the security and integrity of the ballot box, and our office’s efforts to encourage civics education.

One practice of the Lake Oswego Rotary Club is to donate a book to their local public library on behalf of all their speakers. For me, they chose Represent: The Woman’s Guide to Running for Office and Changing the World by June Diane Raphael and Kate Black. Thank you to the Lake Oswego Rotary Club for this wonderful gesture, and thanks to all of Oregon’s Rotarians for their leadership in our communities.


Nineteenth Amendment Centenary
On January 14, 1920, Oregon became the 25th state to ratify the Nineteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which gave women the vote. To commemorate the occasion, the Oregon Women’s History Consortium distributed pins to the Governor, every state legislator, heads of state agencies, and appellate judges and justices.

Many don’t realize it, but Oregon actually led the way in women’s suffrage. In 1912, we removed the requirement that voters be “male” from the Oregon Constitution entirely. The Nineteenth Amendment was ultimately added to the U.S. Constitution on August 26, 1920—which we now observe as Women’s Equality Day. You can learn more about the ratification process in Oregon on [the OWHC website.](http://www.oregonwomenshistory.org/)


Going the Extra Mile
On January 6, Deputy Secretary of State Rich Vial gave me his letter of resignation, which took effect on January 10. In it, he expressed his gratitude for having been given the opportunity to serve as Deputy Secretary of State, and expressed his excitement about the future. You can [read a copy of his letter here.](http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7187499)

Rich has so much to offer government. His thoughtfulness, leadership, and collaborative spirit make him an exemplary public servant, and I have appreciated his advice and assistance during my time as Secretary of State. He went the extra mile every day that we worked together, and I wish him well in his future endeavors.

As always, it is an honor to serve as your Secretary of State.

Sincerely,
Bev Clarno

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Releases Audit of Child Welfare Information System
content:
The Department of Human Services’ (DHS) Child Welfare Information System, otherwise known as OR-Kids, is generally functional for case management, according to [an audit released today](http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7132832) by the Secretary of State. However, auditors found that the system hampered caseworkers’ efficiency due to usability issues, which put data quality at risk.

The findings are outlined in the report entitled: “Oregon’s Child Welfare Information System Is Adequate for Case Management, but Enhancements Are Needed to Improve Usability.”

DHS uses OR-Kids to track Child Welfare casework, including information about children and families who receive services. Auditors found that, while the system provides the basic functionality required for this work, usability challenges make it difficult and time-consuming to find important information about cases. Moreover, these usability concerns increase the risk that data will not be completely or accurately captured in the system.

“Keeping kids safe is the primary concern of Child Welfare in Oregon DHS and the caseworkers need the tools to be able to carry out that objective,” said Secretary of State Bev Clarno. “Having systems that are difficult to use and decrease efficiencies do not help DHS put children first.”

Read the full audit on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/default.aspx

CONTACT: Andrea Chiapella | 503-986-2259 | andrea.chiapella@oregon.gov

headline: Court of Appeals Issues Ruling Regarding Initiative Petition Rejections
subheadline: Oregon Court of Appeals reversed decision of Marion County Circuit Court ruling
content:
Today the Oregon Court of Appeals reversed the decision of the Marion County Circuit Court ruling in November that Secretary of state Bev Clarno was correct in her decision to reject three initiative petitions on the grounds that they violated the single subject rule.
https://www.courts.oregon.gov/news/Pages/coa.aspx

“I am disappointed in the Court’s decision,” said Secretary Clarno. “I believe the single subject rule means something and had hoped the Court would agree. We will have to thoroughly read the opinion and consult with our attorneys before deciding on further action.”

CONTACT: Andrea Chiapella | 503-986-2259 | andrea.chiapella@oregon.gov

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Releases Third Annual Audit Plan for 2020-21
subheadline: 2020-21 Audit Plan for the Oregon Audits Division
content:
Secretary of State Bev Clarno published today the 2020-21 Audit Plan, which includes a full listing of the performance, financial, and IT audits scheduled to be conducted in the coming year: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7132927

This is the third annual audit plan released for the Oregon Audits Division, and the first to be published under Secretary of State Bev Clarno.

“I am proud of the work that the Audits Division does on a daily basis,” said Secretary Clarno. “The audits identified in the 2020-21 Audit Plan took careful consideration and reflect the diverse needs and interests of Oregonians. The Audits Division has proven their skills and professionalism time and time again by identifying ways the state can improve efficiency, save money, or keep Oregonians safe.”

The plan includes a comprehensive list of audits as well as an explanation of the Audits Division’s risk assessment process, a general overview of the division, and a full accounting of the division’s functions, capabilities, and skills.

Read the full 2020-21 Audit Plan on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/default.aspx

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Releases Audit of Travel Oregon Contracting and Compensation
subheadline: Travel Oregon Should Strengthen Controls over Contracting and Improve Accountability of Compensation to Inform Decision Makers
content:
Travel Oregon should strengthen controls over contracting procedures and take steps to share more information regarding the compensation of agency leadership to better inform the Legislature, according to an audit released today by Secretary of State Bev Clarno.

The findings are outlined in the report entitled: “Travel Oregon Should Strengthen Controls over Contracting and Improve Accountability of Compensation to Inform Decision Makers.”
http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7182519

Travel Oregon has generally demonstrated a commitment to ensuring contracts are in place to provide for the needs of the agency. However, the audit makes recommendations to further strengthen contracting procedures. Additionally, the audit highlights the fact that Travel Oregon’s managers are some of the highest compensated managers in state government. Auditors found the agency lacks detailed documentation to support the decisions that led to these high levels of compensation.

“Travel Oregon should improve its transparency and accountability in how it spends taxpayer money,” said Secretary of State Clarno. “Travel Oregon receives funding courtesy of the lodging tax, which is paid by taxpayers in Oregon and visitors alike.”

Read the full audit on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/default.aspx

headline: Office of Small Business Assistance 2019 Annual Report Released
content:
Today, the Secretary of State’s Small Business Advocacy Team released their [Annual Report](https://www.oregon.gov/smallbusiness/Documents/2019-small-business-annual-report.pdf) looking back on their work during 2019. The report provides insight on questions Oregon businesses raise and the challenges they face relating to government regulation.  

The Small Business Advocacy team, within the Secretary of State’s Corporation Division, helps Oregon businesses and nonprofits with 100 or fewer employees. The team, led by Trevor Leahy, help resolve problems, get answers to questions, and navigate Oregon’s regulations at the state and local level. They also connect businesses with technical support in their community.

“Oregon is one of the few states to have a Small Business Advocacy team,” said Secretary of State Bev Clarno. “This is the reason I entered into public service, to help those who are having a difficult time navigating through government regulation, and I am so glad this office is able to serve so many small businesses.”

“Running a small business is tough work and we’re here to help,” said Corporation Division Director and Agency Tribal Liaison Ruth Miles. “We’re fortunate to have a program like this in Oregon.”

“The team is only a phone call, email, or mouse-click away,” said Trevor Leahy, Small Business Ombudsman. The Advocates can be reached at their toll-free number, 1-844-469-5512.

Digital copies of the 2019 Annual Report are available at: https://www.oregon.gov/smallbusiness/Documents/2019-small-business-annual-report.pdf

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Releases Innovative Audit Highlighting the Opportunity for Millions of Dollars in Cost Savings
subheadline: U.S. Treasury’s Do Not Pay Business Center helps to identify, prevent, and recover improper payments in federally funded, state-administered programs.
content:
The Oregon Secretary of State Audits Division found that leveraging a free service from the U.S. Treasury could help identify millions of dollars in potential improper payments and savings in Medicaid and other health and human services programs, according to an audit released today. The findings are outlined in the report entitled: “Using the U.S. Treasury’s Do Not Pay System for Health and Human Services Programs Will Save Taxpayers Money.” http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7182524

The Audits Division was the first state audit organization in the nation to be granted access to the U.S. Treasury’s Do Not Pay Business Center (Do Not Pay). Do Not Pay helps to identify, prevent, and recover improper payments in federally funded, state-administered programs. Our office’s national reputation for performing analytics work was one of the reasons we were the first in the nation to be granted access.

The Audits team identified a process weakness that put Oregon, and the rest of the nation, at risk of automatically renewing Medicaid coverage for a small number of deceased individuals. The audit found that using Do Not Pay would mitigate the risk of improper payments being made on behalf of deceased individuals. By using Do Not Pay to identify ineligible recipients, we identified over $790,000 in savings, generating a return on investment of about $286 in savings for each $1 spent researching data matches.

Auditors also found ways in which Do Not Pay’s data analytics can provide assurance that processes are working effectively. In addition to the return on investment noted above, Do Not Pay identified a data entry error that would have likely resulted in over $6 million in potential improper payments, had the issue not been found.

“The Audits Division continues to lead with innovative audit work, which makes it apparent why they have received national acclaim,” said Secretary of State Bev Clarno. “I am encouraged to see the Department of Human Services and Oregon Health Authority working to gain access to these important tools that will help our state save money and resources. In programs like Medicaid, every penny counts, and I am glad that Do Not Pay has helped identify these savings.”

Read the full report on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/default.aspx

headline: News from the Secretary of State
content:
On January 14, I spoke at a meeting of the Salem Kiwanis Club. Kiwanis is an organization of volunteers who strive to ensure that children grow up in safe and healthy communities. While the international organization works to fight disease and poverty around the world, local clubs focus on benefiting the children in their own communities.

To that end, the Salem Kiwanis Club has been organizing local community service projects since 1921. Sometimes that takes the form of donating books to Head Start schools in Salem. Sometimes it means sponsoring annual spelling bees for local schools. Sometimes (at least once per year) it means raffling off a Mustang at the Oregon State Fair to raise funds for Doernbecher Children’s Hospital! It was a pleasure to join the group for their weekly lunch, where I spoke about the Secretary of State’s role in administering Municipal Audit Law in Oregon, among other things. You can read more in the club’s newsletter [by clicking here.](https://portalbuzzuserfiles.s3.amazonaws.com/ou-15515/newsletters/k%20-%201-14-2020.pdf?AWSAccessKeyId=AKIAJLTDB2YHOAZF5PYA&Expires=1582675200&Signature=WRkNkqJkTth5WnH7aRr7KPQPumk%3d) Special thanks to my friend, former State Representative Bob Repine, for the invitation!
 
Redmond Chamber of Commerce
On January 17, I attended the Redmond Chamber of Commerce’s Annual Awards Banquet, where the chamber recognizes the individuals and businesses that make Redmond such a great community. Among the awards are Citizen of the Year, the Customer Service Award, Businesses of the Year, and the Community Service Award. While there, I visited with Deschutes County Commissioners Tony Debone and Patty Adair.
 
Deputy Secretary of State
On January 22, I swore in Interim Deputy Secretary of State Jeff Morgan. Jeff, who grew up in Condon, has over 39 years of public service experience in the State of Oregon and has served as Director of the Business Services Division since 2004. He’s received national recognition as an exemplary manager and public servant. I’m very pleased that Jeff has agreed to step up to the position of Interim Deputy, and I’m confident that he’ll help guide this agency and assist me in managing the important duties of the office. Welcome, Jeff!
 
Keizer Rotary Club
On January 23, I attended another Rotary Club meeting—this time in Keizer. As I’ve mentioned before, the Rotary Club is a group of business and professional leaders who strive to be involved in their communities by advocating for various humanitarian and ethical causes. I provided attendees with an overview of the various functions of the Secretary of State’s office, including the work of our Elections Division, which you can read more about below.
 
Swearing-In Ceremony
On January 30, I administered the oath of office to Mark Owens, the newest State Representative from House District 60. Covering almost a quarter of the state, including all of Baker, Grant, Harney, and Malheur counties and a portion of Lake County, District 60 is the largest House district in Oregon. Mark and his family own and operate an alfalfa ranch and haying business in Harney County, where he’s served as Chair of his local School Board and as a County Commissioner. As Commissioner, Mark has been involved in shaping land management policy at both the state and federal levels, and I know that his experience and passion for his community will make him a great State Representative. Congratulations, Mark!
 
Elections Security
ICYMI: Op-Ed
On February 2, The Oregonian published an op-ed written by Elections Director Steve Trout. In it, Steve discusses the biggest threat to our elections—misinformation—and offers some advice for how voters can defend themselves from it:
“With unlimited sources of information at our disposal, it can be challenging to recognize what is accurate and trustworthy. Fortunately, there are some simple questions you can ask yourself to help identify misinformation:
“Is there any data or evide
content2:
nce presented to support the statements in question?
“Does this information seem designed to push my political buttons?
“Is there something about this material that doesn’t seem right or too bizarre to be true?
“Who is sharing this?
“Does this individual or group have an agenda?
“One particular risk to voters comes from groups — some well-intentioned, others not — with websites offering to register you to vote or offering other election data. The safest website to register and get election information is that of the Oregon Elections Division: OregonVotes.gov.”

You can [read the rest of the op-ed here.](https://www.oregonlive.com/opinion/2020/02/opinion-an-informed-voter-is-the-best-defense-against-attacks-on-our-democracy.html) It was also published by the [Columbia County Spotlight,](https://pamplinmedia.com/scs/84-opinion/450246-366903-an-informed-voter-is-the-best-defense-against-attacks-on-our-democracy) [The Dalles Chronicle,](https://www.thedalleschronicle.com/guest-commentary-avoid-election-misinformation-in-oregon-s-elections/article_b8b6e86c-4451-11ea-a09c-87b5c767709e.html) and the [East Oregonian.](https://www.eastoregonian.com/opinion/columnists/trout-beware-of-election-misinformation/article_d11acca0-3f08-11ea-ae93-67afc2a9fd5f.html)

Press Conference
On February 4, our office held a joint press conference with the U.S. Department of Justice and the Federal Bureau of Investigation to preview an elections security symposium that we held the following evening in partnership with several other governmental and nongovernmental organizations (see below). Oregon Elections Director Steve Trout remarked on how important it is that government at all levels—county, state, and federal—work together to prevent the spread of misinformation and disinformation regarding our elections. U.S. Attorney for the District of Oregon Bill Williams clarified that, while the symposium wasn’t organized in response to any known threats to our elections security, it was intended to identify ways in which political candidates and campaigns can mitigate potential threats throughout this election cycle and beyond. Finally, FBI Special Agent in Charge Renn Cannon drew attention to the FBI’s [Protected Voices Initiative,](https://www.fbi.gov/investigate/counterintelligence/foreign-influence/protected-voices) which provides tools and resources to help organizations and voters protect against online foreign influence operations and cybersecurity threats. As U.S. Attorney Williams said in response to a question from a reporter, “I can’t think of a more important right to be protected than that of American voters to vote their conscience and beliefs, irrespective of whatever your beliefs are, or party, or non-party.” You can watch a video of the press conference here: https://youtu.be/vSFCSCi_hGo

Symposium
On February 5, our office co-hosted an elections security symposium at which I made welcoming remarks. The target audience for the symposium included county clerks and election officials, as well as campaign managers and communication directors for candidates in upcoming elections. Other speakers included officials from the U.S. Department of Justice, the Oregon Association of County Clerks, the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security, and the Oregon Criminal Justice Division, as well as professionals from Facebook and firms that provide audit, consulting, and risk management services. The symposium lasted two and a half hours and approximately 160 people were in attendance. You can [watch a video of it by clicking here.](https://youtu.be/r4IkbgPHkNI) I want to thank our partners in state and federal government for helping to organize such a successful event. The most effective and efficient way for us to ensure the security of our elections is to work together.
 
Oregon Association of County Clerks
Also on February 5, I attended the Oregon Association of County Clerks’ Mid-Winter Conference in Sal
content3:
em. I gave an overview of the foregoing elections security information, and met with a number of county clerks one-on-one to answer questions. To date, I’ve visited elections offices in 12 of Oregon’s 36 counties, and I am planning on visiting more in the near future. It was nice to see some of the clerks I’ve visited in the past, and to meet others. As always, I am extremely confident in the ability of our county clerks to ensure the integrity of the 2020 election, and I know that the Elections Division of our office will work tirelessly between now and then to make sure that they have everything they need.
 
OR-Kids Child Welfare System
Finally, on February 5, our Audits Division released a report highlighting how difficult it is for case workers to use the state's child welfare database, meaning that crucial information can be overlooked when important decisions are made. In turn, those difficulties lead some case workers to track information on their own—making the system even less reliable. To address these deficiencies, the audit recommends that the Department of Human Services address a backlog of system upgrades and fixes, train employees how to use the system more effectively, implement a plan to ensure that data is collected accurately, and hire a project manager to manage the system upgrade to meet new federal laws. I want to thank our auditors for their quality work and professionalism, and I want to thank the Child Welfare Director for their agency's cooperation and for committing to implement these recommendations by 2021. You can read an Oregonian article [summarizing the audit’s findings here,](https://www.oregonlive.com/politics/2020/02/oregons-hard-to-use-database-on-child-abuse-reports-puts-children-at-risk-audit-finds.html) or you can [read the full audit here.](http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7132832)

 
Going the Extra Mile
Given that the legislature is in session this month, I wanted to recognize my Legislative and Communications Director, Andrea Chiapella. In any given year, thousands of pieces of legislation are introduced in Salem. I believe one of the Secretary of State’s duties is to review those proposals, determine which of them affect the office’s functions, and provide legislators with input regarding those effects. In some cases, it might even be necessary for the Secretary of State to work with legislators to propose new law or amend existing law. To that end, some of Andrea’s primary duties as my Legislative Director include managing legislative affairs and policy development—two things which she is very qualified to do, having spent over a decade working for the Oregon Legislature in both the House and Senate. As if that weren’t enough, she has been involved in efforts to increase diversity, equity, and inclusion in the state government workforce. I feel very fortunate to have Andrea on my staff, and I want to thank her for always going the extra mile.

As always, it is an honor to serve as your Secretary of State.

Sincerely,
Bev Clarno

headline: 2021–2022 Oregon Blue Book Essay Contest
subheadline: Open to all elementary and middle school-aged children
content:
Today, Secretary of State Bev Clarno is pleased to invite all elementary and middle school-aged children to compete in the 2021–2022 Oregon Blue Book Essay Contest.

The 2021–2022 Blue Book theme is 100 Years of Oregon’s State Parks. Our state parks display Oregon’s natural beauty, and that they exist is a testament to what Oregonians value. Next year we will celebrate the 100th anniversary of the opening of Oregon’s first state park and the new edition of the Oregon Blue Book.

Essays should answer the following question: What is your favorite Oregon State Park and why? Students are also encouraged to illustrate their essays with drawings.

The Oregon Blue Book, published biennially since 1911, is the official state almanac and fact book. "The Oregon Blue Book has been a state resource for more than 100 years,” said Secretary of State Bev Clarno. “It is filled with great information on over 2,000 areas of interest that make-up Oregon including everything from aviation to Pulitzer Prize winning Oregonians. It’s a great resource and, frankly, a fun read with breathtaking photos of Oregon.”

Contest winners’ entries will be included in the 2021-2022 edition of the Oregon Blue Book, and the winners will be invited to the Capitol for the official Blue Book release party and celebration in early 2021.

To see contest information and details visit: [Oregon Blue Book Essay Contest.](https://sos.oregon.gov/blue-book/Pages/explore/exhibits/essays-2021/enter-contest.aspx) Questions? Contact Theresa Rea with Oregon State Archives at 503-378-5168 or Theresa.Rea@oregon.gov.

The deadline for submitting entries is October 22, 2020. Each essay must be identified with the student’s name, grade, teacher’s name, school’s name and address, and be mailed (not emailed) to:
Theresa Rea
Oregon State Archives
800 Summer St. NE
Salem OR 97310

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Releases Municipal Summary Report for Fiscal Year 2018
content:
Today, Secretary of State Bev Clarno released the [Municipal Summary Report for fiscal year 2018.](http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7182849) This report summarizes the financial reporting of Oregon’s municipalities and their compliance with Municipal Audit Law.

The Secretary of State administers Municipal Audit Law (ORS 297.405-.555 and .990) with the purpose of enforcing and elevating transparency which keeps Oregon municipalities accountable to their citizenry.

“We often talk about improving government at the state level, but I am equally committed to ensuring accountability at the local level,” said Secretary of State Bev Clarno. “Administering Municipal Audit Law, and publishing this annual report, are important steps to ensuring local municipalities are properly accounting for taxpayer money and held accountable.”

The Municipal Summary Report is prescribed in statute. It includes information about the timeliness of reports filed, deficiencies noted in audits, and whether required communications and corrective action plans were filed as required. Municipal corporations include counties, cities, school districts, and special districts.

Read the full report on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/recent.aspx

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Releases Audit of the Statewide Facility Planning Process
subheadline: DAS has improved the statewide facility planning process by enhancing data collection and reporting
content:
The Department of Administrative Services (DAS) has improved the statewide facility planning process by enhancing data collection and reporting, but the process could be further improved to more effectively inform decision-makers and increase its transparency, according to an audit released today by the Secretary of State: http://records.sos.state.or.us/ORSOSWebDrawer/Recordpdf/7227892

The findings are outlined in the report entitled: “DAS Should Build on Recent Enhancements and Further Improve the Facility Planning Process to Better Inform Investments in State Facilities.”

Since 2013, DAS’s efforts have increased both the quality and consistency of facility data used to inform investments in the state’s $7 billion facility portfolio. Auditors found further improvements could be made to the way that DAS prioritizes facility investments and shares planning information with the Legislature and the public. Additionally, while there have been improvements in statewide facility data collection, further funding is needed to ensure that DAS and other agencies are able to accurately monitor the condition of the state’s facility portfolio.

“DAS is well-positioned to take the lead in developing a plan that guides future investments in state-owned facilities,” said Secretary of State Bev Clarno. “These facilities are an important and valuable asset, and we want to be sure there is as much transparency as possible.”

Read the full report on the Secretary of State website: https://sos.oregon.gov/audits/Pages/recent.aspx

CONTACT: Andrea Chiapella | 503-986-2259 | andrea.chiapella@oregon.gov

headline: Filing Deadline is Tuesday
subheadline: Tuesday, March 10th is the deadline to file as a candidate for the 2020 Primary
content:
Next week, 5 p.m. on Tuesday, March 10th is the deadline to file as a candidate for the 2020 Primary held on May 19th. Becoming a candidate for public office is challenging, but an important part of our democratic process. You can file online or in person at our office (255 Capitol St. NE, Suite 501, Salem OR 97310) up until the 5 p.m. deadline.

“I commend anyone who is willing to sacrifice their time and energy to become a candidate for any office, it is tough work and takes a strong dedication to service,” said Secretary Bev Clarno.

The May 19th Primary is only 11 weeks away. April 28th is the last day to register to vote or to change your party affiliation [oregonvotes.gov/myvote](https://sos.oregon.gov/voting/Pages/myvote.aspx)

For more information about running for public office visit our website https://sos.oregon.gov/elections/Pages/runforoffice.aspx

headline: May Primary Moves Forward as Planned
content:
Oregon’s May 19th Primary Election will continue as planned. The certified list of 371 candidates that filed to run for office has been sent to our county election experts this week so that they can begin laying out the ballots to be sent to the printer. The voters’ pamphlet is also being put together and proofread so it can be sent to the printer later this month.

Ballots for military and overseas voters will be mailed out not later than April 4th, and regular ballots will be mailed out starting April 29th.
Because Oregon votes by mail we do not have to be concerned about social distancing issues at polling places that so many other states are struggling with. Many states are looking to implement our vote by mail system as a safer way to conduct elections in November. Contingency plans are being prepared to deal with any impacts the COVID-19 virus may have on our election processes. 

The May 19th election is a primary election. Primaries serve two main purposes. The first is for all voters to be able to cast ballots for candidates for nonpartisan offices like judges and some county and other local offices. The second is for the voters registered with a major political party to select their nominees for partisan office like US President, Oregon Secretary of State, Treasurer and Attorney General and legislators at both the state and national level.

Those registered as not affiliated with a political party, or registered with a minor party (Constitution, Independent, Libertarian, Pacific Green, Progressive, Working Families) will receive a ballot that includes only nonpartisan offices. The US Supreme Court has ruled that political parties get to decide who votes in their primaries so those of you registered as not affiliated with a political party or registered with a minor party will not have candidates for President or any partisan office on your May primary ballot. You will have them on your November 3rd ballot. Only registered Republicans will be able to vote to choose their nominees for partisan offices, and only registered Democrats will be able to vote to choose their nominees for partisan offices.

If you desire to register to vote, update your registration, or change your political party, the deadline is April 28th but please do it as soon as possible.  As always, go to your trusted source of election information at [oregonvotes.gov](https://sos.oregon.gov/voting-elections/Pages/default.aspx)

headline: Secretary of State Bev Clarno Appoints Cameron Smith as Chief of Staff
content:
Secretary of State Bev Clarno has selected Cameron Smith to serve as her Chief of Staff. Smith has previously served as Director of the Oregon Department of Veterans’ Affairs and as Director of the Department of Consumer and Business Services. He left state government in 2019, when he filed to run for Secretary of State, but withdrew his candidacy before the March 10 filing deadline. A veteran of the United States Marine Corps, Smith served three tours of duty in Iraq and attained the rank of captain. Military service runs in Smith's family: his great-grandfather was Fleet Admiral Chester Nimitz, a major figure in the naval history of World War II. A graduate of Carleton College in Minnesota, Smith lives in Southeast Portland with his wife and two young children.

"I am delighted that Cameron Smith has agreed to serve in the Secretary of State's office" said Clarno. "During his time in state government, he earned a reputation for intelligence, integrity, and effectiveness. In these times of extreme partisanship, the fact that a Republican is appointing a Democrat to serve as her Chief of Staff may surprise some, but it reinforces my belief that the duties of the Secretary of State's office—overseeing fair and secure elections, conducting thorough audits of state government agencies, helping Oregon's small businesses, and maintaining Oregon’s important records—are duties that should not and must not be considered as partisan. Cameron's experience and knowledge of state government will be very helpful as my office works through the challenges of the Coronavirus pandemic to provide the best service possible to all Oregonians."

Smith said, "I am honored that Secretary of State Clarno has asked me to return to government service. I have long admired her trailblazing career dedicated to fiscal responsibility and all Oregonians. I also appreciate her commitment to bipartisanship—I learned in the Marine Corps that you don't care what party the individual is next to you, you just care that they do their job and they are part of the team.”
Smith will join the office on March 30, 2020.

CONTACT: Andrea Chiapella | 503-986-2259 | andrea.chiapella@oregon.gov
